  4. Prioritizing Profits: Losing positions are selected only after all profitable positions have been exhausted. These subtle differences reflect each exchange's risk management philosophy. Binance's detailed calculation of effective leverage takes into account the entire account's state. Hyperliquid's ratio-based approach is mathematically elegant. OKX's multi-trigger system is more sophisticated, going beyond simple capital exhaustion. These formulas ensure that the most leveraged, profitable positions are deleveraged first. In other words, those who take the greatest risk to achieve the highest returns pay the price for the system's stability. The mechanism is both brutal and elegant. Centralized Exchanges (CEX) vs. Decentralized Exchanges (DEX): Two Distinct Approaches to ADL. The implementation of ADL reveals profound philosophical differences between centralized and decentralized exchanges. After analyzing both during the October Crisis, the contrast between the two is striking.

    ADL on Centralized Exchanges: A Black Box Operation

    Centralized exchanges implement ADL with minimal transparency, creating what I call an “uncertainty premium” in trader behavior:

    • Opaque Triggers: Insurance fund levels and depletion thresholds remain strictly confidential

    • Internal Execution: ADL occurs within private matching engines with no external visibility

    • Limited Reporting: Only selective, often sanitized, data is released after an incident

    • ADL on decentralized exchanges: Transparency is a double-edged sword

      Decentralized exchanges implement ADL through smart contracts, which achieves complete but inflexible transparency:

      • Public parameters: All trigger conditions and ranking formulas are visible in the code

      • Real-time visibility: Every ADL event is immutably recorded on the blockchain

      • Immutable rules: Smart contracts can be executed without human intervention

      • Provably Fair: Anyone can audit whether ADL strictly adheres to established rules. Hyperliquid's performance in October highlighted both the strengths and limitations of this approach. They recorded 35,000 auto-dilution (ADL) events from 20,000 users, building an unprecedented dataset for analysis. But this transparency also revealed the system's ruthless nature—smart contracts are unable to identify when they could destroy carefully constructed hedged portfolios. Pros and Cons of ADL After analyzing thousands of ADL events since October, I can offer a nuanced perspective on this controversial mechanism.

        Arguments for ADL

        Preventing Exchange Insolvency: Without automatic deleveraging (ADL), exchanges face insolvency when losses exceed capital. Otherwise, an exchange's collapse would mean total losses for all users. ADL at least maintains platform operations and allows trading to resume.

        Maintaining Market Integrity: By preventing the accumulation of bad debts, ADL ensures that the zero-sum nature of perpetual futures remains mathematically intact. For every dollar lost, there is a corresponding gain within the system.

        Providing Risk Transparency: While imperfect, the five-level ADL indicator system allows traders to assess their deleveraging risk in real time. This, at least in theory, enables dynamic position management. Can Optimize Exit Timing: Counterintuitively, data from October shows that ADL actually helped many short positions by forcing liquidations near market bottoms. Without ADL, many short positions might have remained open during the rebound, reducing profits. Arguments Against ADL: Penalizing Success: ADL explicitly prioritizes the most profitable traders. This creates a perverse incentive structure where excessive success becomes a liability. It's like penalizing the best students in order to help those who are failing. Undermining Portfolio Hedges: The most damaging impact occurs when ADL disrupts carefully constructed hedging strategies. A profitable short position that hedges long exposure, if closed through ADL, could trigger cascading losses across the entire portfolio. Time-blind operation: Even with indicators, ADL can activate within minutes during periods of extreme volatility. During October, traders switched from a Level 1 to a Level 5 trading light in 300 seconds, faster than human reaction time. Socializing private losses: At its core, ADL forces profitable traders to bear the losses of others' losing positions. This violates the fundamental principle of individual responsibility in the markets. Did ADL help or hurt the October crash? The data reveals a more complex story than initially reported. My analysis shows that ADL is both a savior and a destroyer, often simultaneously. Where ADL Helps: An analysis of Hyperliquid's transparent data reveals a striking fact: ADL actually increases the profit and loss (PnL) of the vast majority of short positions. The reason? Timing. 35,000 ADL events were concentrated in a 5-minute window (21:16-21:21 UTC), coinciding with the bottoming out of most asset prices. Bitcoin's forced liquidations at $102,000, while seemingly painful at the time, became spectacular when the price rebounded to $108,000 within hours. Platform solvency was fully protected. Despite the largest liquidation event in cryptocurrency history, no major exchange failed. The HLP vault even saw a $40 million profit in a single day, demonstrating that providing liquidity during a crisis can be highly profitable even with widespread auto-deleveraging. The system averted a worse outcome. Without auto-deleveraging, cascading bankruptcies could have frozen the market for days or even weeks. This mechanism enabled normal trading to resume within hours of the peak of the crisis. Where ADL hurts: The real damage came from the market structure failures that necessitated auto-deleveraging (ADL). Between 20:40 and 21:35 UTC, market makers orchestrated what I can only call a coordinated sell-off. Market depth plummeted 98%, from $1.2 million to just $27,000. This wasn't a panic—rather, it was a calculated act of self-preservation by institutions who believed 87% of their positions were long and knew exactly what was coming. ADL became a cascading amplifier by destroying portfolios. Consider a documented case: a trader held a $5 million long position in BTC (3x), a $500,000 short position in DOGE (15x) as a hedge, and a $1 million long position in ETH (5x). The DOGE short position was highly profitable and leveraged, so it was the first to be liquidated by ADL. Unhedged, the BTC and ETH positions were liquidated minutes later. Total portfolio loss: 100%. The infrastructure collapse exacerbated the situation. As one analyst noted, "Cascading liquidations overwhelmed servers with millions of requests. Market makers were unable to place bids in a timely manner, resulting in a liquidity vacuum." The feedback loop between auto-deleveraging (ADL) activation and infrastructure failures caused unprecedented disruption.

        Designing a Better ADL

        Based on the empirical evidence from this crash, here are some suggestions on how the ADL system should be improved:

        1. Gradual ADL with time delay

        Replace immediate position reduction with gradual warnings:

        • T-60 seconds: Issue warnings to high-priority positions, showing current queue position

        • T-30 seconds: Provide an opportunity to voluntarily reduce positions at the current market price

        • T-0: Force only positions that have not been reduced voluntarily ADL

        This preserves the autonomy of traders while ensuring the solvency of the system.

        2. Market maker obligations

        The voluntary liquidity provision model completely failed in the crisis. Future market structures require:

        • Binding obligations to maintain minimum quotes during periods of stress

        • Enhanced rebates and privileges linked to performance during periods of stress

        • ADL protections for market makers who maintain liquidity during crises

        • Penalties for withdrawals during systemic stress events

        3. Dynamic insurance requirements

        Static insurance fund ratios have proven insufficient. Dynamic sizing should be based on the following factors:

        • Position concentration indicators (87% long bias is a clear warning)

        • Cross-margin exposure leverage

        • Correlation risk of packaged assets

        • Real-time market maker participation level

        4. Portfolio-aware ADL

        Current ADL mechanisms are position-insensitive and undermine hedges indiscriminately. A better design should:

        • Identify hedging relationships before auto-deleveraging

        • Provide portfolio-level ADL that maintains hedge ratios

        • Allow traders to specify protected hedge pairs

        • Implement smart ADL to minimize disruption to the overall portfolio

        5. Hybrid Transparency Model

        Combine the efficiency of a centralized exchange (CEX) with the accountability of a decentralized exchange (DEX):

        • Publish real-time insurance fund levels Display the statistical distribution of ADL queues Implement on-chain verification of ADL event execution Allow traders to purchase ADL insurance or prioritize protection ADL is a mirror of market maturity The October 2025 crash showed that ADL is neither a pure villain nor a pure hero, but rather a mirror reflecting the structural weaknesses of the cryptocurrency market. When market makers fled, infrastructure collapsed, and 87% of positions were skewed in one direction, ADL became the only mechanism to prevent a complete system collapse. The surprising finding is that ADL actually helps most short positions by forcing liquidations at the optimal time, suggesting that the mechanism itself is flawed, not so much in the system that requires it. In a well-functioning market with loyal liquidity providers, a robust infrastructure, and balanced positions, the ADL mechanism should rarely be activated. The path forward is not to eliminate ADL, but to create markets where it is no longer urgently needed. This requires fundamental changes:

          • Market makers who can’t abandon their jobs during a crisis

          • Infrastructure that scales with pressure, not volume

          • Risk management to prevent extreme position imbalances

          • Transparency that builds trust even during coercive actions

          Until these reforms are implemented, ADL will remain a necessary evil—a mechanism that violates free-market principles to preserve the market’s viability. The October crash demonstrated that, with $19 billion to $40 billion in liquidations, the choice was not between ADL and fairness but between ADL and total collapse. Perhaps the true lesson is that pure individual freedom is an illusion in leveraged markets connected by shared margin pools. When losses exceed gains, someone pays the price. The ADL simply determines who bears the cost. Data from October 2025 suggests we are slowly learning this lesson. But given historical precedent, it remains to be seen whether we will remember it when greed triumphs over fear again. This article combines on-chain data, exchange reports, and market microstructure analysis of the events of October 10-11, 2025. The contrast between transparent DEX operations and opaque CEX reporting highlights the ongoing challenges of comprehensive market analysis. I would like to thank the researchers who provided the detailed ADL data that made this level of analysis possible. The opinions expressed in this article are my own, inspired by the relevant information, and do not represent any entity.

data.title: When the market complained about CZ, people began to miss SBF.
data.description:
Author: @amuse Compiled by: AididiaoJP, Foresight News

SBF's story doesn't end in the courtroom where he was sentenced. It continues in spreadsheets, balance sheets, and forgotten ledgers.

These documents tell a very different story from the one the public hears.

Through the prison's slow, monitored email system, and through friends, I corresponded with SBF. In his letters, he appeared calm, analytical, and reflective. He read voraciously in science fiction, immersed in fictional worlds, perhaps because in the real world, the company he founded was dismantled by lawyers who understood neither cryptocurrency nor the fundamentals of business. He had lost weight and become quieter. What he has to say now is worth listening to, because the official narrative surrounding FTX's collapse may be one of the most egregious distortions of financial truth in recent memory. According to data provided by SBF, FTX was never insolvent, not when he handed over control under legal and regulatory pressure in November 2022, nor when the market bottomed out. By his calculations, at the time of the bankruptcy filing, FTX had $15 billion in assets and only $8.4 billion in liabilities. He argues that this massive difference should have served as a safety net to protect customers and creditors. Yet bankruptcy lawyers declared the company "irretrievably bankrupt" and sold off its assets at fire-sale prices. Today, every creditor has been repaid in full or even received additional benefits, but billions of dollars that could have been recovered were wasted on exorbitant attorney fees, legal battles, and decisions made by people who had “no idea what they were doing.”

The mainstream narrative portrays FTX as a house of cards, a madcap enterprise run by a founder who behaved erratically, commingled funds, and defrauded investors. But the deeper truth is much more complicated.

FTX's operating model is similar to that of many fast-growing technology startups: rapid iteration and rapid trial and error in areas where the law is still underdeveloped. SBF now admits that the irony is that the company's real weakness was not fraud but compliance. They spent too much energy satisfying regulators who only knew how to punish but not guide.

This involves an ethical distinction that is rarely discussed publicly. SBF emphasizes that taking responsibility is not the same as admitting guilt. Responsibility means choosing to be the leader of events rather than the passive one, and facing up to one's role in their formation. In this spirit, he does not deny his errors of judgment. He deeply regrets: giving up control when leadership was most needed; failing to effectively monitor Alameda's risk exposure; and focusing too much on regulatory games while neglecting operational management. But he also refuses to accept the reputation of being a "villain who stole billions of dollars." He believes that his real failure lies in leadership, not theft.

The contrast couldn't be starker: Had FTX been left to recover naturally, it could have become one of the most remarkable comeback stories in financial history. Instead, it became a bonanza for professional services firms, which were handsomely compensated for handling its "failure." The lawyers' hasty disposal of assets at the market's trough prevented them from capitalizing on the subsequent cryptocurrency rebound, which could have multiplied the value of their assets. Sam estimates that the bankruptcy estate could have generated an additional $125 billion in value with better management. Instead, they sold at the bottom, gloating about their existing solvency and paying their own hefty bills with funds provided by the very stakeholders they were supposed to protect.

data.title: Getting rich is a war of attrition: even geniuses can be defeated by greed
data.description:
These spectacular booms and busts are nothing new throughout history. The only difference is that now, with the internet, these events are discovered and amplified in seconds. Some people have assets exceeding $30 million and ended up with nothing. You know who else went bust? Isaac Newton—in the famous South Sea Bubble. The Game of Attrition—Avoiding Zero We can apply this to everything. People give up by the time they turn 25, indulging in cheap alcohol and bar shots. They work hard at starting a business for three months at most and then settle for annual salary increases that barely keep up with inflation. This even applies to dating. Most men either let things slide or refuse to travel abroad to find their ideal type (usually an attractive foreigner). Life, after all, isn't a sprint. While you'll experience huge step-change leaps, the foundation is laid during those seemingly stable months or even years. It's a long, brutal race of attrition (think of those boring games on the TV show "Survivor" where the only thing you have to do is stand still on a pole). It's a test of who can stand tall while others burn out, quit, or self-destruct.

What's different about this game and those shows? You never have to go back to zero. You may have a bad year, month, or quarter. But that's okay. As long as you never go back to zero, there's no financial reset.

Every game worth playing (business, investing, fitness, relationships, etc.) has an unwritten rule: The winner isn't the fastest or strongest runner in a given year, but the one who survives a decade of constant change.

The recent crash

There are many such stories. How many of you have forgotten the following: 1) Sam Bankman-Fried—a thirty-year-old billionaire who ran one of the most profitable trading platforms of the time. His greed led him to embezzle client funds, ultimately leading to bankruptcy and imprisonment. 2) Do Kwon—who once ridiculously declared, "Hold on, guys, we're putting in more money," essentially doubling down on his Ponzi scheme and ultimately going bankrupt. 3) Long-Term Capital Management—a fund literally founded by a Nobel Prize winner. An overly leveraged bet at the wrong time? Goodbye. Even Isaac Newton lost his mind during the South Sea Bubble. After selling early for a profit, he jumped back in (at the peak) out of jealousy. He lamented, "I can calculate the motion of celestial bodies... but not the madness of human nature."

This is a lesson repeated for centuries. The plot is the same, the technology changes, but the outcome is exactly the same: greed, leverage, and an inability to stay in the game.

The world's smartest people have become entangled in their complex models, forgetting that it is emotional humans, not numbers, that drive the market.

A war of attrition is the core strategy: this game rewards those who can overcome their own psychological weaknesses.

For decades, Buffett has been derided as "boring." He underperforms during periods of mania, consistently buying things that no one wants. Yet, he remains victorious. His empire compounded relentlessly because he had only one rule: don't lose money. While we'd never ask him anything technical, his strategy is conceptually sound. All you have to do is stay in the game and refuse to sell when you're losing (which requires deep research, diamond-like determination, and zero leverage—the interest alone can cause you to lose money). Jeff Bezos did the same thing. He raised $1 million by selling less than 20% of his company. He could have sold more, but he wanted to maintain control—stay in the game. It proved to be his smartest decision. Through the dot-com crash and years of heated board meetings, he remained firmly in control. By trading greed for endurance, he ultimately built an empire (Amazon) worth over a trillion dollars. Elon Musk has been through it all too. He's not the best example, as he could have gone bankrupt. However, he remains a brilliant example of survival. After PayPal, he plowed nearly all his fortune into Tesla and SpaceX, at one point teetering on the brink of bankruptcy. He borrowed money from friends to pay his rent, but never gave up. He fought to survive, not to appear successful. Apple is another (surprising) example. In the 1990s, it faced bankruptcy. Most companies would have collapsed, but not Apple. They cut costs, rebuilt their business, and waited patiently. A few years later, the iPod arrived, followed by the iPhone, and the compounding phase began. This is another trillion-dollar lesson in resilience. In short, think carefully about all these examples before you decide to use leverage. Could you survive a true recession? A -50% to -60% drop? Losing your job? Adding an emergency expense? It's much easier to make model predictions through rose-colored glasses. Never make an investment you regret. Any investment you've thoroughly researched and believe in should be held forever, say, for 20-30 years. This doesn't mean you should hold all of it, but you should always keep some. If you bought Bitcoin at $100 and sold it all at $200, you'd never be able to live with yourself again. We know plenty of people who've done that. There are those who bought Ethereum at $80, sold it all at $200, and watched it rise to over $4,000, while their S&P 500 underperformed by over 1,000% during that time. In short, with any well-researched investment, you can sell some of it. But be sure to keep some in case you sell too soon. Choosing to go to zero is pointless. Most people aren't even crushed by leverage; they crush themselves. They sell after a stock or token has risen 10x, proud of having "locked in profits." They sell their startup for a few million dollars for peace of mind, only to watch it later go public and be worth millions or even billions (Victoria's Secret is a famous example). They abandon careers, relationships, or ideas before the exponential curve bends upward. The inflection point is where all the gains are! (Reminder: progress is nonlinear, and this applies to stocks, tokens, and any small business). To avoid this, you need to structure your life to absorb losses while retaining some high-risk positions that have already risen. Keep your expenses low and maintain sufficient liquidity to make large bets without feeling desperate. Hold on to your skills and your equity. These two assets will continue to compound until the inflection point in the curve appears.

Endure the boredom that precedes the breakthrough, and accept looking wrong for years. Successful people aren't those with the best strategies... they're those who stick with them long enough until they start to work.

Every major success story is a case study in painful endurance and conviction. Compound interest only works for the few who can stay solvent long enough to experience it. It's your ticket to the top 1%.

Most people can't stand being undervalued or delaying gratification. They need recognition and attention, so they cash out to feed their egos. They need comfort, so they forgo future possibilities. They mistake busyness for progress and the approval of others for security. They voluntarily quit the game before it turns in their favor. Ironically, focusing on survival and adaptability itself creates inevitable success. If you stay in the arena long enough, the odds will eventually tilt in your favor. The longer you can endure pain, boredom, and obscurity, the more exposure you have to favorable "tail events." Life can't stop someone who refuses to leave. If you're in that "grinding" phase where it seems like you're getting nowhere, remember that it's entirely psychological. If you're making the right choices, momentum is quietly building. You shouldn't expect immediate rewards; you should focus on survival. For every month you stay above the breaking point, every year you hold your ground, your chances of a breakthrough increase. This is the mathematics of attrition. People hate cockroaches, but they teach us a valuable lesson: survive at all costs. The more you want results today, the less you'll get. This is how Lady Luck works. The skill you really want is staying power. Stay at the table long enough, and Lady Luck will eventually think, "Hmm, this guy really doesn't seem to care, so I'll give him the pot of gold." Don't let yourself get crushed. Don't prematurely cash out of investments with the potential for asymmetric returns. Don't trade your future for comfort. Keep your expenses low, your ego smaller, and your (bankroll) runway longer. Because life doesn't reward the smartest player—it rewards the one who perseveres until the final table. Think of it like playing poker. Your goal is to reach the final table, not to dominate the field on day one. Survive long enough, and statistics will do the rest. A final note on survival—even if you screw up. If you need some real-world examples of comebacks, here are a few names you'll recognize today: Disney: In 1923, Disney's Laugh-O-Gram Films went bankrupt after its distributors failed to pay. He moved to Hollywood, created Mickey Mouse and Snow White, and built an empire.

Ford: He went bankrupt twice before founding Ford Motor Company. His first company, Detroit Motors, collapsed due to overly expensive and inefficient products. He later invented mass production, transforming manufacturing.

Jobs: Fired from Apple in 1985, he devoted himself to NeXT and Pixar, teetering on the brink of financial ruin. Apple ultimately acquired NeXT in 1997, allowing Jobs to return…with the iMac, iPod, and iPhone.

George Foreman: The renowned boxer went bankrupt in the late 1970s but returned to the ring at age 38. Once he had earned enough money, he created the Foreman Grill, which earned him hundreds of millions of dollars.

Milton Hershey: Before founding Hershey Chocolate, he had two failed candy businesses. Even the most recognizable brands had founders who failed multiple times, but they just kept trying.

There are thousands of other stories we know about, and millions more we don't. The principle is the same. You learn from other people's mistakes. Don't go to zero, don't sell 100% of an investment you firmly believe in, and don't use leverage.

On the rare occasion that you do mess up... well, others have come out of it worse. It just doesn't make sense to put yourself in that position, given the outsized returns expected in tech, cryptocurrencies, and niche internet businesses in 2025.

That's just a cartoon's perspective, though.

data.title: Web3 Entrepreneurship Discussion: Do Crypto Projects Really Need to Be Open Source?
data.description:
In the world of Web3, open source has almost become a form of "political correctness." Decentralization, transparency, and verifiability are concepts that are naturally tied to "open code." If a blockchain project is not open source, it often finds it difficult to gain the trust of the community. Investors may also question: If you say you are decentralized, why are you hiding it?

This concept did not emerge out of thin air. It is a continuation of the "open source movement" on the Internet.

From Linux in the 1990s, to Apache and MySQL in the 2000s, and then to Android in the mobile Internet era, open source software has gradually established a new consensus: code should belong to all mankind, not to a single company. In 2009, when Bitcoin was born, Satoshi Nakamoto directly opened the source code on SourceForge. Without the endorsement of an auditing firm or government approval, the only trust came from the fact that anyone could download the code, run a node, and verify transactions. Consequently, open source became a norm in the Web3 world. However, open source means much more than transparency. It means that any developer can fork, improve, and submit code. Many well-known Web3 projects have used open source to establish a "technical consensus" with their communities and developers. The most typical example is Ethereum. Its open source code has not only been directly reused, spawning hundreds of EVM-compatible chains, but has also attracted countless developers to join as contributors, driving continuous upgrades to the protocol itself. For example, in 2015, community developer Fabian Vogelsteller proposed EIP-20 (later known as ERC-20), laying the foundation for tokens and becoming the cornerstone of DeFi's prosperity. Another example is EIP-721, proposed by Dieter Shirley (CryptoKitties' chief architect) and others in 2017, which has become the foundational standard for NFTs. Even more remarkable is EIP-1559, initiated by community researcher Eric Conner and others, which introduced disruptive improvements to the transaction fee mechanism and influenced Ethereum's economic model. It can be said that it is precisely because of open source that Ethereum continues to attract new developers, allowing "external contributions" to gradually become "industry standards," creating a true network effect. However, the problem is that open source is not a free lunch. It brings not only trust and shared development, but also means "lowering barriers to entry, diluting moats," and naked competition. When you expose the underlying code to the public, competitors can copy it at almost zero cost. This is how countless "Ethereum killers" were born throughout history: from EOS to Tron, and later, a series of EVM-compatible chains, most of them directly reused Ethereum's codebase, making only adjustments to consensus mechanisms, performance parameters, or funding subsidies. The same story also plays out at the application layer. The most typical example is the DeFi summer of 2020. Uniswap pioneered a new paradigm for decentralized trading with its AMM model, but because the contracts were completely open source, SushiSwap was able to directly copy the code with almost no R&D costs and add additional liquidity mining incentives. In just a few days, over $1 billion in liquidity was drained from Uniswap, and the market briefly speculated that SushiSwap would completely replace it. Facts have proven that while open source lowers the barrier to innovation, it also amplifies vampire competition. Web3 projects need not only technological leadership but also continuous development in brand, ecosystem, and governance. Otherwise, code alone will not be enough to create a defensible defense. This leads to a paradox: the more open a project, the more likely it is to spawn competitors; conversely, the more closed the code, the harder it is to win the trust of users and investors. So, what should Web3 entrepreneurs do? Portal Labs believes the following points may provide support. First, treat open source as a starting point, not an end point. Open source code can attract developers and communities, but it's the ongoing development that truly determines a project's value. Ethereum's ability to navigate cycles isn't due to the open source nature of the EVM, but rather to its supporting systems: the EIP proposal mechanism, foundation support, and a long-term developer community. For Web3 entrepreneurs, open source is merely a stepping stone; their moat lies in governance, ecosystem, and network effects.

Second, focus on differentiation.

The reason why Uniswap ultimately maintained its position was not because its code was unique, but because it continued to build its own barriers beyond open source. It continuously launched V2 and V3 iterations, and was the first to introduce centralized liquidity design, improving the efficiency of capital utilization; through its foundation and governance mechanisms, it attracted developers and the community to form long-term ties; more importantly, Uniswap has become the "default entry" for DeFi, and its brand recognition and compliance exploration have given it an irreplaceable position. For Web3 entrepreneurs, if they choose open source, they must consider: If others copy the code, can they replicate the product experience, community atmosphere, compliance structure, and partnerships? These are often the factors that truly determine long-term survival. Third, use open source as leverage. Open source isn't just about risk; it can also amplify potential. Many projects adopt a "layered open source" approach: the underlying protocol is open source to facilitate community collaboration, while the core algorithm or application layer remains closed source, creating a balance. For example, DYDX's underlying matching logic is open source, but its risk control and core matching engine remain closed source to ensure differentiation. Some teams use open source as a business expansion tool, lowering the barrier to entry and allowing others to drive ecosystem growth. Typical examples include the Cosmos SDK and Polkadot Substrate. Their fully open-source underlying frameworks have attracted countless public chain projects to build upon their ecosystems, ultimately feeding back "other people's innovation" into their own network effects. Open source is both a cornerstone of trust in Web3 and a double-edged sword for entrepreneurs. It can attract developers and foster an ecosystem, but it can also amplify competition and dilute competitive moats. However, open source isn't a question of "if" or "if," but rather "how." Truly astute Web3 entrepreneurs will neither blindly close themselves off nor naively open up everything. Instead, they will find their own path between trust and competition. If you decide to open source, here's Portal Labs' list of recommended open source tools: Clear boundaries: Foundational code, such as the protocol layer and smart contracts, should be fully open source to establish transparency and trust. Business algorithms, matchmaking logic, and risk control models can remain closed source to create unique advantages.

It is recommended to manage separate repositories on GitHub, mark the open source license agreement (such as MIT, GPL), and maintain independent closed-source internal modules.

  1. Institutionalized co-construction

Learn from Ethereum's EIP model and establish a proposal process (PR → Review → Community Voting).

You can set up a public Discord/forum and set up a "Proposal Submission" section; hold a community call to review proposals once a month.

  1. Quick iteration

Open source means others can copy the code, so your moat depends on the speed of iteration.

It is recommended to establish a rhythm of "one small version every two weeks and a large version every three months" and publish the Roadmap on the official website or GitHub Project.

  1. Brand and Trust

In the open source world, technology can be copied, but trust cannot. The real barriers often come from community atmosphere and compliance exploration.

It is recommended to treat security audits, compliance endorsements and community activities as "basic skills", such as

  • regularly publish audit reports;

  • invite law firms to issue compliance opinions;

  • keep users and developers in the ecosystem through hackathons and developer conferences.

  1. Ecological leverage

Use open source as a tool to attract developers and businesses, rather than simply "donating code."

For example:

  • Provide SDK/templates to lower the threshold for secondary development;

  • Establish a Grant program to fund applications based on your protocol;

  • Achieve integration with three to five key ecological partners (wallets, exchanges, data platforms).

data.title: US SEC Chairman: Cryptocurrency and tokenization are SEC’s “top priorities”
data.description:
Author: Martin

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission is shifting from law enforcement and supervision to framework building, charting a clear development path for the crypto industry.

"Cryptocurrency and tokenization are the SEC's top priorities." Paul Atkins, Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), made it clear at the Fintech Week held in Washington, D.C. recently that in the face of this industry that has long been in a regulatory gray area, Atkins changed his previous serious attitude as a regulatory agency and half-jokingly called the SEC the "Securities and Innovation Commission." This statement signals a possible major shift in U.S. regulatory policy for crypto assets. Atkins emphasized that the SEC hopes to build a strong regulatory framework to attract crypto talent and companies that have left the United States to return and lay the foundation for future innovation and development. In fact, the SEC has already begun to promote specific measures. Atkins revealed that it will launch an "innovation exemption" mechanism to enable companies to bring on-chain products and services to market faster.

New regulatory thinking: from law enforcement to framework building

For a long time, the U.S. SEC has adopted a regulation through law enforcement” approach to the cryptocurrency industry. This strategy has repeatedly caused controversy, but the current SEC leadership has shown a different governance approach - building an adaptive regulatory framework rather than blindly curbing it.

Atkins pointed out that the SEC will launch an "innovation exemption" program, with the goal of creating a system similar to a "super application" to enable multiple regulatory agencies involved in crypto assets to work together and avoid the trouble of companies having to repeatedly register across multiple departments.

The background of this change is that the urgency of cryptocurrency regulation is becoming increasingly prominent. In recent years, the scale of the crypto market has expanded rapidly, and various tokenization practices have continued to emerge.

The global regulatory attitude is undergoing a major shift from strict risk control to regulatory guidance. In his speech, Atkins also emphasized his optimism about distributed ledger technology, calling it "the most exciting part of the crypto field." This statement indirectly reflects the SEC's recognition of the fundamental value of blockchain technology, rather than a complete denial of the potential contribution of cryptocurrencies. "Project Crypto": Strategic Layout of the US Market The SEC's proactive shift is not an isolated incident, but part of the US strategic layout for crypto assets. Earlier this year, the SEC launched Project Crypto, a comprehensive reform of securities rules aimed at updating securities rules and regulations to enable the US market to migrate to the blockchain.

The priorities of this initiative are clear: Provide certainty about the securities attributes of crypto assets; ensure that entrepreneurs can raise funds on-chain without facing endless legal uncertainty; and allow "super application" type trading platforms to innovate.

At the same time, according to the recent report of the President's Digital Asset Markets Working Group, the SEC will work with other agencies to ensure that platforms can provide trading, staking and lending services for crypto assets under a single regulatory framework. Atkins believes: "Regulation should provide the 'minimum effective dose' of regulation required to protect investors, and no more than that."

Real challenges: government shutdown and market volatility

However, the SEC's efforts to promote crypto regulation face practical obstacles. The US government shutdown has entered its second week, and Congress has failed to reach a funding agreement, which has greatly restricted the actions of federal agencies and put SEC employees on unpaid leave.

This stagnation may affect the SEC's ability to respond to unexpected events in the crypto market. For example, just recently,the SEC announced plans to take legal action against Ripple for selling XRP, causing XRP to plummet 30% that day and other major cryptocurrencies to fall sharply.

In its lawsuit, the SEC accused Ripple of conducting an "unregistered securities offering," while Ripple argued that XRP should be considered a currency rather than a security.

This type of dispute highlights the urgency of establishing a clear regulatory framework.

Digital Currency Competition from a Global Perspective

The U.S.'s crypto regulatory measures are even more strategically significant in a global context. Currently, the global digital currency field is gradually diverging into three main paths:the central bank digital currency path represented by China,the "cryptocurrency + stablecoin" path represented by the United States, and

the diversified path represented by the European Union.

The United States is attempting to further consolidate the U.S. dollar's global currency status in the digital economy era by buildinga crypto-dollar hegemony anchored by strategic Bitcoin reserves and using U.S. dollar stablecoins as a means of circulation.

The EU's Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (MiCA) recently came into effect, establishing a comprehensive regulatory framework for digital assets.Some European policymakers have called for the formulation of "MiCA 2" to cover decentralized finance, non-fungible tokens, and digital asset lending.

Against this international competitive landscape, the active actions of US regulators are not only a need for domestic financial regulation, but also part of the global competition for monetary sovereignty. As Atkins said,"the era of encryption has arrived,"and the question now is how countries respond to this trend.

The next few months will be a critical period for the reform of U.S. crypto regulation.If the SEC can quickly advance the "Crypto Plan" and "Innovation Exemption" after government funding is restored, the United States may attract a large number of crypto companies and talent to return.And new products such as tokenized stocks, prediction markets, and early token issuance may also gain broader development space.

As major economies such as the European Union and Singapore accelerate the establishment of digital asset regulatory frameworks, the landscape of global crypto competition is taking shape.The U.S. SEC's policy shift will become a key variable affecting the future digital asset landscape.

data.title: Are stablecoins really viable? Paypal minted 300 trillion on Ethereum
data.description:
In the early morning of October 15th, Beijing time, the US stablecoin and payment giant Paypal caused a big mistake:

Cointelegraph also stated: Congratulations to PayPal for becoming the richest company on the planet, with assets of 300 trillion US dollars... This lasted for about 20 minutes, until Paxos burned its newly minted PYUSD stablecoin worth 300 trillion US dollars. Many people may not have any idea of ​​what $300 trillion means. You should know that the GDP of all countries in the world combined in 2024 will only be $100 trillion. With this operation, Paypal directly issued all the wealth created on Earth in the past three years!

Fortunately, the blockchain ledger cannot be tampered with. I've listed the transfer hash of this historic event here for interested friends to see~

Minting Hash:

0xc45dd1a77c05d9ae5b2284eea5393ecce2ac8a7e88e973c6ba3fe7a18bf45634

Destroy Hash:

Strictly speaking, PYUSD (PayPal USD) is a compliant US dollar stablecoin issued by the US fintech company Paxos Trust Company and supported by the PayPal brand.

Therefore, it can be said that the real issuer of PYUSD is Paxos Trust Company, which was founded in 2012, is headquartered in New York, and holds a trust license from the New York State Department of Financial Services (NYDFS).

Paxos is also a company that claims to be compliant:

  • is one of the few stablecoin issuers approved by official US regulators;

  • is responsible for issuing, clearing, and custodial US dollar reserves;

  • regularly publishes audit reports to ensure that there is a real US dollar or equivalent government bond behind every PYUSD. Paxos previously issued the BUSD stablecoin for Binance, but was ordered to cease issuance by the New York Department of Financial Services in 2023. Since then, it has shifted its focus to partnering with compliant brands, such as PayPal. It's no surprise that Paxos is the most knowledgeable blockchain provider in the traditional financial world. It helps banks and payment giants move US dollars onto blockchain while ensuring regulatory compliance. PayPal is one of the world's largest electronic payment platforms, with over 400 million users. In the past few years, it has gradually laid out its presence in the field of encrypted payments:

    The core selling point of PYUSD is "compliant, secure, and regulated." Although this blunder was merely a technical issue, it exposed:

    • the risk of human error in contract operations;

    • the issuer's transparency mechanism is not perfect;

    • the communication mechanism between PayPal and Paxos is somewhat slow (destruction within 20 minutes).

    This is a negative signal for institutional investors and the traditional financial community.

    Especially since the crypto market just experienced an extreme market crash like 1011 a few days ago, and now stablecoin issuers under the banner of compliance have created sky-high wealth out of thin air

    They may be even more skeptical:

    "Are you crypto people reliable?"

data.title: Cryptocurrency VCs are almost gone
data.description:
In April 2025, ABCDE, a well-known crypto VC firm founded by Du Jun, announced it would halt new investment and its second-phase fundraising. This once-active investment firm shifted its focus to post-investment management and exit arrangements for existing projects, epitomizing the current state of crypto VC. In 2024, we reported on a wave of activism by crypto VCs. At the time, a group of senior partners shed their VC aura and invested in project developers or the secondary market, all because of one simple statement: "VC isn't profitable." A year later, the bull market truly arrived. Bitcoin continues to hold steady at $100,000, and Ethereum has returned to $4,000, with occasional celebrations of get-rich-quick deals in the secondary market. However, when the spotlight turns to the primary market, crypto VCs face an even tougher time than in the previous round. Instead of making money, they're burdened with infamy. Within the ecosystem, they're suppressed by exchanges, market makers, and project owners. Their investment logic is completely shattered by the collapse of their narratives. They can't raise funds and face criticism that their impact isn't as good as that of influencers (KOLs). Crypto VC, where is it going? What's happening with crypto VC? In the last cycle, crypto VCs were accustomed to making quick bets. They chased narrative trends, willing to invest in projects that didn't yet have a product or even a complete team. As long as the story was compelling enough, LPs and the secondary market would pay. That was an era when "storytelling was more important than building a product." However, as we enter 2024–2025, this logic has suddenly broken down. So, what's happening with the once-active Asian crypto VC scene now? RootData data shows that compared to 2024, the number of primary market investments by Asian crypto VCs has plummeted since 2025. For example, SevenX Ventures, the three most active crypto VCs in the previous cycle, made its last public investment in December 2024. Foresight Ventures' investments plummeted from 54 to 5, and HashKey Capital's investments also dropped from 51 to 18. Among the top 10 most active investment institutions in 2024, OKX Ventures topped the list with 72 investments, but this number had shrunk significantly to 12 in 2025. According to Jack, a partner at a crypto VC firm, the current crypto VC landscape is severely fragmented. Small and medium-sized VCs are facing particular difficulties, and many are being forced to pivot.

He gave his own observations:

Between 2023 and 2025, about 5-7% of crypto VCs will turn to marketing/KOL agency business;

About 8-10% of crypto VCs will turn to incubation/post-investment driven institutions, and the size of the post-investment team will expand by 30-50%;

The response of most institutions is to turn to the secondary market, or extend the fund cycle, reduce management costs, or even pursue compliant exit paths such as ETF/DAT/PIPE. In other words: VCs have become service providers, or simply "the big spenders." Former crypto VC investor Mark put it bluntly: "Nowadays, purely primary investment institutions are practically committing suicide." LD Capital has transitioned to the secondary market, with founder Yi Lihua becoming ETH's "milk king" and maintaining a strong presence. Furthermore, some crypto VCs are being "forced" into AI investment. As early as March, IOSG founding partner Jocy posted on social media that another project in her portfolio was transitioning to AI. As more and more crypto investors discovered an inexplicable increase in AI entrepreneurs in their portfolios, they were forced to vote with their feet. For example, Bixin Ventures significantly reduced its investments in the crypto industry, opting instead to invest in emerging AI companies such as IntelliGen AI, which focuses on AI healthcare. While this transformation can be considered a proactive self-help measure, some institutions have simply announced a halt to investments. ABCDE, the renowned crypto VC firm founded by Du Jun, announced in April 2025 that it would cease new investment and its second-phase fundraising, focusing instead on post-investment management and exit arrangements for existing investments. "ABCED was relatively honest, publicly announcing its closure, but many other crypto VCs are keeping it a secret," commented a VC practitioner interviewed. With the sharp decline in investment volume, the underlying paradigm of the current primary crypto market is shifting. In Jack's words, this is a shift from "liquidity-driven narrative speculation" to "cash flow and compliance-driven infrastructure development." Over the past few years, crypto VC investment logic has relied heavily on narrative. However, funding data for 2024–2025 shows a clear shift: According to Pitchbook data, global crypto/blockchain VC funding totaled only $1.97 billion in Q2 2025, a 59% decrease from the previous quarter, reaching its lowest point since 2020. Furthermore, late-stage funding accounts for over 50% of total funding, indicating that investors are more focused on mature projects with real revenue and verifiable cash flow. "Racing for narrative-driven early-stage projects has become more difficult, while projects that can generate revenue and profits (such as exchanges, stablecoin issuers, and RWA protocols) are more likely to attract capital," said Dashan, partner at Waterdrop Capital. Furthermore, the "listing effect" of leading exchanges has been significantly reduced this time around. In the past, simply being listed on a major exchange would bring valuation liquidity. However, since 2025, despite an increase in the number of Binance listings, the impact on secondary valuation premiums has weakened. According to CoinGecko data, the average 30-day decline for new coins following their TGE in the first half of the year exceeded 42%. At the same time, new exit paths have emerged, such as compliant ETFs/tokenized access funds (DATs), or structured secondary liquidity projects like protocol buybacks and ecosystem funds. "This shift doesn't mean 'speculation' has disappeared, but rather that the speculative window has shortened, with beta returns giving way to alpha screening," said Jack. The current predicament of crypto VCs can be simply summed up: no profit. Crypto analyst KK frankly admits that the primary issue is that crypto VCs currently occupy a relatively backward position within the cryptocurrency ecosystem. The lockup period for a project for VCs is 1-3 years. However, due to the rapidly changing narratives in the crypto industry, by the time the lockup is unlocked, the hype surrounding these narrative-building projects may have faded, with token prices plummeting, even approaching zero. Some projects have even been declared dead before they even make it to an exchange. Furthermore, many crypto VCs invested in too many overvalued projects during the previous cycle. This current cycle's logic has been disproven, and actual revenue and other data simply don't support the high valuations. "At the time, many VCs invested in overseas projects at high prices, partly because they believed higher valuations offered greater stability, and partly because investing alongside renowned international investment institutions would enhance their brand reputation. But now, it seems many have lost money," said KK. Most importantly, crypto VCs lack bargaining power. "Essentially, they can only provide money," Mark said. One interviewee even bluntly stated, "In this market, VC money is less valuable than a Twitter influencer." What do projects need most? It's not just money, but liquidity. Market makers can create depth in the secondary market, listing a token on an exchange directly determines whether a project can access liquidity, and influencer calls can help projects sell their tokens faster to cash out... These liquidity participants often take the cheapest chips first, then resell them to VCs at valuations several times higher. The result: crypto VCs pay the most but receive the worst prices. Thus, an absurd phenomenon has emerged: crypto VCs have become the group with the least bargaining power in the crypto market. They cannot compete with exchanges, market makers, or even KOLs. The "capital kings" of the primary market have become the "end of the ecosystem chain" in the crypto industry. The Fundraising Dilemma If "not making money" is the survival dilemma of VCs, then "not being able to raise funds" is a life-or-death crisis. According to PitchBook data, global crypto VC financing totaled only $1.97 billion in Q2 2025, a 59% quarter-over-quarter decline. This stands in stark contrast to the peak of over $10 billion in a single quarter in 2021. Many traditional LPs are no longer investing. Besides experiencing losses after not seeing returns from their previous round of investments, they also believe there are "easier ways to make money in the cryptocurrency world," Dashan explained. "For example, buying mainstream coins, DeFi mining, and options arbitrage all offer average returns of over 30%. This makes it difficult to convince LPs to invest in VC, which can take years to exit and likely result in losses." Furthermore, financial backers are also changing. Jack observes that traditional dollar-denominated LPs are shrinking, replaced by Middle Eastern sovereign funds like Mubadala and QIA, as well as Asian family offices. Many in Singapore and Hong Kong, in particular, are allocating to crypto secondary and early-stage equity through FOs and multi-strategy funds. However, these emerging LPs are more discerning: they want real cash flow, not just PowerPoint presentations; they demand compliant custody, auditing, and fund licenses to avoid regulatory scrutiny; and they prefer hybrid funds that combine secondary and primary funds, allowing for the short-term realization of partial returns. Cruelly, money is increasingly concentrated in a small number of top investors. "Unless they possess strong vertical differentiation or key resources, small and medium-sized funds find it even more difficult to attract LPs," said Jack. The fundraising dilemma is particularly critical for crypto-native VCs. On the one hand, they need to continuously raise funds from outside sources, but on the other hand, they lack the resources of industry collaboration to empower them. VCs with backgrounds in exchanges or market makers, or those using their own capital, not only have the money but also the industry resources, giving them greater confidence to secure bargains. However, crypto-native VCs have to navigate this life-or-death dilemma. To put it more bluntly: in this market, LPs have no shortage of investment opportunities; what they lack is certainty, which crypto-native VCs cannot provide. Where can we break through? While the current state of the primary market is dire, those still involved firmly believe this is merely a period of pain. Once the reshuffle is complete, only those who remain at the table will reap the fruits of success. They remain optimistic about the future. "The current transformation holds new opportunities," Dashan said. "For example, stablecoins. Some predict that their issuance will exceed $3 trillion. The settlement, clearing, and compliance services surrounding this $3 trillion will inevitably create a wave of new assets. This presents an opportunity for crypto VCs to prepare for the future." The broader narrative is equally alluring. According to the Citi GPS 2024 report, the scale of tokenized assets is projected to reach $10–16 trillion by 2030. Both on-chain settlement platforms and the issuance of real-world assets (RWAs) offer entry points for VCs. "With each cycle, new opportunities emerge around new assets, whether it's trading platforms, financial derivatives, or innovative DeFi projects, all injecting vitality into the market," said Mark. However, if crypto VCs want to survive in this game, they must completely reshape their role. They can move beyond the role of pure financial investors to provide market making, compliance, and liquidity support, and even directly participate in project operations. This model is more like an "investment bank" than a traditional VC. Alternatively, they can create structured funds and, through financial engineering techniques like DATs, PIPEs, and SPACs, design diverse exit paths for LPs, transforming "uncertain narratives" into "predictable cash flows." They must also develop genuine research and data capabilities, focusing on quantifiable metrics like on-chain revenue, user retention, and protocol fees, rather than continuing to bet on the next "empty narrative." These directions may be the last bargaining chips for crypto VC. However, the irony of history is that those who truly survive are often those who survive the most challenging circumstances. Crypto VC's "weightless era" may be nurturing the birth of the next star. After all, only those players who are still standing on the ruins are qualified to welcome the next bull market.
